config_files.notes: Primary graphics profile. The live files were observed in Documents; some XML exports may still contain a CONFIGPATH value that points elsewhere on OneDrive-backed installs.
Mirrors startup/render flags such as dx12.
Legacy/alternate graphics mirror. Treat the XML file as the source of truth for active graphics tuning.

keys.effect: Enables the DirectX 12 renderer.
keys.notes: PCGamingWiki reports DX12 can stutter in The Division on select systems. On the RTX 4070 SUPER test system it did not improve the benchmark, so DX11 is the safer default.

keys.effect: Dedicated contact shadows from sun, moon, and many artificial lights.
keys.notes: All High looks best but costs several FPS; All Low is the best compromise when chasing 90+ FPS on ultrawide.

keys.effect: Post-process anti-aliasing for alpha edges, particles, and other transparent detail.
keys.notes: Temporal AA is always on and does most of the work; PostFx AA is a subtle extra pass. Ultra costs more than it usually looks like it should.

keys.effect: Resolution of the game's volumetric fog and haze.
keys.notes: High is close to Ultra visually; Medium is the first sensible cut if FPS needs to recover.

keys.effect: How much distant world detail streams in and how far the engine tries to keep it loaded.
keys.notes: Mixed HDD/NVMe Storage Spaces can amplify the cost of aggressive streaming values. On the test system, 7 was the practical quality/performance balance; 8 is only worth it if the game remains smooth on the mixed pool.

keys.effect: Level of detail and mesh complexity for distant objects such as bridges, road barriers, and city geometry.
keys.notes: This is the main fix for low-poly distant bridges. 16 was too low at 5120x1440; 17 is the first useful quality bump without pushing the benchmark too far from the 90 FPS floor.

keys.effect: Peripheral colour-fringing post effect.
keys.notes: Keep off for clarity; it has no gameplay value and is a common eye-strain trigger.

keys.effect: Shadowing technique used for world shadows.
keys.notes: HFTS is a GTX 900 Maxwell-only path. GTX 8xx cards should not expect HFTS; use High or PCSS+ instead. RTX 40-series cards should use PCSS+ if the option is available.

keys.effect: Shadow map detail for the selected shadow technique.
keys.notes: Keep High if you're using High or PCSS+ shadows; lower values are visibly softer and can make contact points float.

keys.effect: Screen-space reflection quality for wet streets, windows, and reflective surfaces.
keys.notes: Very High is the cleanest look, but High is the usual sweet spot when you still want 90+ FPS.

keys.effect: Ambient occlusion technique for contact shadows and scene depth.
keys.notes: NVIDIA HBAO+ is the most accurate option and was worth keeping on the high-end RTX 4070 SUPER test system.

keys.effect: Texture filtering quality.
keys.notes: Very low cost; keep maxed. The visual improvement is obvious at ultrawide resolutions and the performance cost is tiny.

keys.effect: Temporal anti-aliasing mode.
keys.notes: Stabilization is only worth using if you are chasing every last frame and can tolerate a little more shimmer; Supersampling is usually the right pick even on high-end GPUs.

keys.effect: In-game VSync.
keys.notes: Use VRR/G-Sync instead.

keys.effect: Limiter mode for the custom frame cap.
keys.notes: Pair with Custom Fps to keep frame pacing stable on the mixed storage pool.

keys.effect: Custom frame-rate ceiling in FPS.
keys.notes: 108 is the practical cap for the current 90+ FPS target on this ultrawide; 120 is only worthwhile if you later recover more headroom.

keys.effect: Internal render scale.
keys.notes: Keep native at 100% on this RTX 4070 SUPER / 5120x1440 display pair. Lowering view scale should be a last-resort fix only.

keys.effect: Adds cinematic blur and bokeh in cutscenes, aiming down sights, and Echoes.
keys.notes: For motion comfort, override this to Off. NVIDIA notes the gameplay difference between Medium and High is subtle, so this is a good comfort setting to trim first if you feel eye strain or nausea.

keys.effect: Darkens the corners of the image.
keys.notes: Keep Off for cleaner edges and lower tunnel-vision sensation. The effect is subtle, so there is little reason to leave it enabled if motion comfort matters.

keys.effect: Upper bound of the in-game mouse sensitivity slider range.
keys.notes: Defines the slider ceiling, not the actual sensitivity you play at. The live value is set via the in-game Controls menu and is not stored as a readable config key. Increase this value if the default maximum (80) feels too low.

keys.effect: Lower bound of the in-game mouse sensitivity slider range.
keys.notes: Defines the slider floor. The default of 2 is already very low; rarely needs adjusting.

manual_only_settings.reason: Motion blur is a primary nausea trigger. No verified motion-blur config key was found in the live files, so this is the first comfort toggle to disable if the menu exposes it.

manual_only_settings.location: In-game gameplay or accessibility menu (if exposed)
manual_only_settings.reason: Impact-driven camera jolts and scripted shake can trigger nausea even when the FPS is stable. No verified config key was found in the live files.

manual_only_settings.recommendation: Set to the widest comfortable value
manual_only_settings.reason: A wider FOV reduces tunnel-vision sensation and often helps with motion sickness. On ultrawide, keep it as wide as you can comfortably tolerate without distortion.

manual_only_settings.location: In-game Gameplay or Controls menu (if exposed)
manual_only_settings.reason: Walking-camera oscillation is a primary motion-sickness trigger. No config key was found for head bob in any Division file (GFXSettings XML, state.cfg, graphic settings.cfg) — this setting is in-game menu only, and may not be separately exposed in this game.

notes: Tom Clancy's The Division runs on the Ubisoft Snowdrop engine. See knowledge/game-engines/snowdrop.json for engine-level defaults.

On the tested RTX 4070 SUPER / 32 GB / 5120x1440 system, benchmark runs stabilized around 83-85 FPS average with 87-89 typical FPS, indicating persistent streaming and main-thread pressure rather than only first-run shader compilation.
The mixed HDD/NVMe Storage Spaces pool magnifies the cost of aggressive streaming settings. Extra Streaming Distance and Object Detail are the main levers for distant pop-in and bridge LOD quality.
Object Detail 16 was too low for distant bridge geometry at 5120x1440; 17 is the first useful quality bump.

Motion-comfort pass: no verified motion-blur, head-bob, or screen-shake config key was found in any Division config file (GFXSettings XML, state.cfg, graphic settings.cfg). Use the in-game menu to disable motion blur, camera shake, and head bob if those options are exposed; depth of field, chromatic aberration, and vignette can all be turned off via config.

Mouse sensitivity: maxMouseSensitivity and minMouseSensitivity in state.cfg define only the slider range bounds (defaults: 80 and 2). The actual sensitivity value is set exclusively via the in-game Controls menu and is not readable from the config files.

sources.label: NVIDIA GeForce - Tom Clancy's The Division Graphics & Performance Guide
